adams_williamson_equation.name: Adams-Williamson Equation
adams_williamson_equation.caption:
Can you name this equation? Hint: it's how we know what's inside the Earth without ever digging.
Drop your guess in the comments before you swipe — no peeking 👉

ANSWER: Adams-Williamson Equation

This equation describes how pressure and density vary with depth inside Earth, helping us understand our planet's interior structure. It's the foundation of geophysics and seismology, revealing what lies beneath our feet.

advection_equation.name: Advection Equation
advection_equation.caption:
Do you know this one? Hint: it tracks how pollution drifts on the wind and salt rides ocean currents.
Comment your answer first, then swipe to slide 2 to check yourself 👉

ANSWER: Advection Equation

Watch how quantities transport through space! The advection equation models everything from pollutant dispersion in air to salt movement in ocean currents. It's essential for weather prediction and environmental science.

alfven_velocity.name: Alfvén Velocity
alfven_velocity.caption:
Name that equation. Hint: it sets the speed of magnetic waves rippling through a plasma.
Type your guess below before you swipe — no cheating, we'll know 👀

ANSWER: Alfvén Velocity

Named after physicist Hannes Alfvén, this equation describes the speed of magnetic waves in plasma—crucial for understanding solar flares, auroras, and fusion reactor behavior. Plasma physics gets wild!

ampere_law_v1.name: Ampère's Law (Version 1)
ampere_law_v1.caption:
What's this equation called? Hint: electric currents create the magnetic fields inside every motor.
Commit to an answer in the comments, then swipe to see if you nailed it 👉

ANSWER: Ampère's Law

One of Maxwell's legendary equations! Ampère's Law connects electric currents to the magnetic fields they create. Without it, we'd have no motors, generators, or understanding of electromagnetism.

ampere_law_v2.name: Ampère's Law (Version 2)
ampere_law_v2.caption:
Know this one? Hint: how magnetic fields curl around a current-carrying wire, point by point.
Guess in the comments before checking slide 2 — how many can you get? ✅

ANSWER: Ampère's Law

The differential form of Ampère's Law reveals how magnetic fields curl around electric currents at every point in space. This is the mathematical language Maxwell used to unify electricity and magnetism.

angular_frequency.name: Angular Frequency
angular_frequency.caption:
Today's challenge: Hint: it counts oscillations in radians per second — the natural clock of waves.
Say it out loud, drop it in the comments, then swipe to check 👉

ANSWER: Angular Frequency

Express oscillations in radians per second instead of cycles per second! Angular frequency ω simplifies the mathematics of waves, vibrations, and quantum systems. It's the natural unit for periodic motion.

angular_impulse.name: Angular Impulse
angular_impulse.caption:
Guess the equation. Hint: a twist applied over time — it's how you change how fast something spins.
Comment your guess before you swipe — bonus points for the units 🤓

ANSWER: Angular Impulse

Just as force creates linear momentum, torque creates angular momentum through angular impulse. This principle explains why spinning ice skaters pull their arms in to spin faster—conservation of angular momentum at work!

angular_jerk.name: Angular Jerk
angular_jerk.caption:
One equation, one guess. Hint: the third time-derivative of an angle — robots need it for smooth motion.
Drop your guess in the comments before you swipe — no peeking 👉

ANSWER: Angular Jerk

The third derivative of angle with respect to time! Angular jerk measures how rapidly angular acceleration changes, essential for understanding rigid body dynamics and robotic motion control. It's pure calculus in motion.

angular_momentum_cross_product.name: Angular Momentum (Cross Product Form)
angular_momentum_cross_product.caption:
Can you name this equation? Hint: the conserved quantity that keeps a spinning top upright, written as r × p.
Comment your answer first, then swipe to slide 2 to check yourself 👉

ANSWER: Angular Momentum (Cross Product Form)

L = r × p: Angular momentum emerges from the cross product of position and momentum vectors. This vector quantity defines the amount of rotation in a system and is conserved in isolated systems.

angular_momentum_rotational.name: Angular Momentum (Rotational Form)
angular_momentum_rotational.caption:
Do you know this one? Hint: why an ice skater speeds up the instant they pull their arms in.
Type your guess below before you swipe — no cheating, we'll know 👀

ANSWER: Angular Momentum (Rotational Form)

L = Iω connects rotational inertia to angular velocity. Understand why compact spinning objects rotate faster—it's the rotational analog of F=ma, governing all spinning motion from atoms to galaxies.

angular_velocity.name: Angular Velocity
angular_velocity.caption:
Name that equation. Hint: it measures spin rate in radians per second, from centrifuges to planets.
Commit to an answer in the comments, then swipe to see if you nailed it 👉

ANSWER: Angular Velocity

How fast does something spin? Angular velocity ω measures rotation rate in radians per second. It's fundamental for understanding everything from planetary orbits to centrifuges to quantum mechanical angular momentum.

archimedes_principle.name: Archimedes' Principle
archimedes_principle.caption:
What's this equation called? Hint: the 2,000-year-old reason ships float and hot-air balloons climb.
Guess in the comments before checking slide 2 — how many can you get? ✅

ANSWER: Archimedes' Principle

The buoyant force equals the weight of displaced fluid—an ancient insight that explains why ships float and hot air balloons rise. This principle has shaped engineering for over 2000 years!

arrhenius_equation.name: Arrhenius Equation
arrhenius_equation.caption:
Know this one? Hint: why a small rise in temperature can make a chemical reaction dramatically faster.
Say it out loud, drop it in the comments, then swipe to check 👉

ANSWER: Arrhenius Equation

This exponential equation governs reaction rates and temperature dependence—tiny changes in temperature cause huge changes in reaction speed. Understanding it is key to chemistry, catalysis, and industrial processes.

barometric_formula.name: Barometric Formula
barometric_formula.caption:
Today's challenge: Hint: why the air runs out long before you reach the top of Everest.
Comment your guess before you swipe — bonus points for the units 🤓

ANSWER: Barometric Formula

Why does air get thinner as you climb? The barometric formula describes exponential pressure decay with altitude—explaining why mountaineers need oxygen at high elevations and why satellites eventually fall.

bekenstein_hawking_entropy.name: Bekenstein-Hawking Entropy
bekenstein_hawking_entropy.caption:
Guess the equation. Hint: a black hole's disorder, written on its surface area.
Drop your guess in the comments before you swipe — no peeking 👉

ANSWER: Bekenstein-Hawking Entropy

Black holes have entropy proportional to their surface area—mind-bending physics at the intersection of gravity and thermodynamics. This equation hints at deep connections between black holes and information theory.

bernoulli_equation.name: Bernoulli's Equation
bernoulli_equation.caption:
One equation, one guess. Hint: why faster-moving fluid has lower pressure — the secret of aeroplane wings.
Comment your answer first, then swipe to slide 2 to check yourself 👉

ANSWER: Bernoulli's Equation

Energy conservation in flowing fluids! Bernoulli's equation explains why faster-flowing water has lower pressure—the principle behind airplane wings, carburetors, and water jets. Fluid dynamics masterpiece.

bohr_model.name: Bohr Model Equation
bohr_model.caption:
Can you name this equation? Hint: the first equation to predict hydrogen's electron orbits and their energies.
Type your guess below before you swipe — no cheating, we'll know 👀

ANSWER: Bohr Model Equation

Niels Bohr cracked the atomic code with this equation describing electron orbits in hydrogen. Though superseded by quantum mechanics, it beautifully shows how discrete energy levels arise from quantum rules.

boltzmann_distribution.name: Boltzmann Distribution
boltzmann_distribution.caption:
Do you know this one? Hint: what fraction of molecules have a given energy at temperature T.
Commit to an answer in the comments, then swipe to see if you nailed it 👉

ANSWER: Boltzmann Distribution

What fraction of molecules have energy E at temperature T? Boltzmann distribution answers this, governing everything from gas molecules to stellar atmospheres. It's the statistical foundation of thermodynamics.

boltzmann_entropy.name: Boltzmann Entropy
boltzmann_entropy.caption:
Name that equation. Hint: disorder as a count of microstates — it's carved on its author's gravestone.
Guess in the comments before checking slide 2 — how many can you get? ✅

ANSWER: Boltzmann Entropy

S = k ln(Ω): Entropy measures disorder through the logarithm of possible microstates. Boltzmann's insight connects microscopic randomness to macroscopic thermodynamics—one of physics' deepest ideas.

borda_carnot_equation.name: Borda-Carnot Equation
borda_carnot_equation.caption:
What's this equation called? Hint: the energy a flowing fluid loses when a pipe suddenly widens.
Say it out loud, drop it in the comments, then swipe to check 👉

ANSWER: Borda-Carnot Equation

Energy loss in fluid flows strikes again! The Borda-Carnot equation quantifies head loss when flowing fluid encounters sudden area changes. Critical for designing pipelines, dams, and water treatment systems.

born_rule.name: Born Rule
born_rule.caption:
Know this one? Hint: how a wavefunction becomes the probability of what you'll actually measure.
Comment your guess before you swipe — bonus points for the units 🤓

ANSWER: Born Rule

The Born rule is quantum mechanics' interpretation—the probability of measuring outcome equals the squared amplitude of the wavefunction. It's how randomness enters our universe at the deepest level.

capacitance.name: Capacitance
capacitance.caption:
Today's challenge: Hint: charge stored per volt — your phone's touchscreen senses you with it.
Drop your guess in the comments before you swipe — no peeking 👉

ANSWER: Capacitance

C = Q/V: How much charge can a capacitor store per volt? Capacitance is everywhere in electronics—from smartphone screens to power systems. This simple ratio defines a crucial electrical property.

capacitive_reactance.name: Capacitive Reactance
capacitive_reactance.caption:
Guess the equation. Hint: why one circuit component blocks DC but waves high frequencies straight through.
Comment your answer first, then swipe to slide 2 to check yourself 👉

ANSWER: Capacitive Reactance

In AC circuits, capacitors resist current flow with frequency-dependent reactance Xc = 1/(ωC). Higher frequencies flow through easily while DC blocks completely—the foundation of AC circuit analysis and filter design.

cauchy_momentum_equation_v1.name: Cauchy Momentum Equation (Version 1)
cauchy_momentum_equation_v1.caption:
One equation, one guess. Hint: Newton's second law, rewritten for every little parcel of a flowing fluid.
Type your guess below before you swipe — no cheating, we'll know 👀

ANSWER: Cauchy Momentum Equation

Newton's second law for fluids! This equation extends F=ma to continuous media, describing how pressure gradients and body forces accelerate fluid elements. It's foundational for computational fluid dynamics.

cauchy_momentum_equation_v2.name: Cauchy Momentum Equation (Version 2)
cauchy_momentum_equation_v2.caption:
Can you name this equation? Hint: how internal stresses push a fluid around — the engine of weather simulations.
Commit to an answer in the comments, then swipe to see if you nailed it 👉

ANSWER: Cauchy Momentum Equation

The stress tensor form reveals internal forces in flowing fluids—how pressure and viscous forces combine to create motion. This equation runs every weather simulation and aerodynamic calculation.

centrifugal_force.name: Centrifugal Force
centrifugal_force.caption:
Do you know this one? Hint: the outward 'push' you feel on a merry-go-round that isn't really there.
Guess in the comments before checking slide 2 — how many can you get? ✅

ANSWER: Centrifugal Force

Feel the outward push in a spinning reference frame? Centrifugal force appears as a pseudo-force in rotating coordinates—useful for analyzing motion in carousels, hurricanes, and rotating machinery.

centripetal_acceleration.name: Centripetal Acceleration
centripetal_acceleration.caption:
Name that equation. Hint: the inward pull, v²/r, that keeps anything moving in a circle.
Say it out loud, drop it in the comments, then swipe to check 👉

ANSWER: Centripetal Acceleration

Keep it together: a = v²/r is the inward acceleration needed to maintain circular motion. Whether it's gravity holding planets or friction keeping cars on curves, centripetal acceleration is always working.

charles_law.name: Charles' Law
charles_law.caption:
What's this equation called? Hint: warm a gas at constant pressure and its volume grows in lockstep.
Comment your guess before you swipe — bonus points for the units 🤓

ANSWER: Charles' Law

V/T = constant for gases at constant pressure: volume and temperature are proportional. This fundamental gas law explains why hot air rises and why tire pressure increases when heated.

continuity_equation.name: Continuity Equation
continuity_equation.caption:
Know this one? Hint: mass can't vanish — it's why a river speeds up through a narrow gorge.
Drop your guess in the comments before you swipe — no peeking 👉

ANSWER: Continuity Equation

Matter can't vanish: ∂ρ/∂t + ∇·(ρv) = 0 expresses conservation of mass in fluid flow. Faster flows in narrow pipes is a direct consequence—what goes in must come out!

coulombs_law.name: Coulomb's Law
coulombs_law.caption:
Today's challenge: Hint: the inverse-square push between two charges — electricity's answer to gravity.
Comment your answer first, then swipe to slide 2 to check yourself 👉

ANSWER: Coulomb's Law

The electrostatic force between charges: F = kq₁q₂/r². This inverse-square law governs atomic forces, chemical bonding, and the entire electromagnetic framework. Elegant, powerful, foundational.

crackle_fifth_derivative.name: Crackle (Fifth Derivative)
crackle_fifth_derivative.caption:
Guess the equation. Hint: position, differentiated five times — it shares a name with a breakfast-cereal mascot.
Type your guess below before you swipe — no cheating, we'll know 👀

ANSWER: Crackle (Fifth Derivative)

Beyond jerk comes crackle—the fifth time derivative of position! While rarely used in physics, it appears in high-precision trajectory planning and demonstrates the beauty of calculus's higher derivatives.

dalembert_operator.name: d'Alembert Operator
dalembert_operator.caption:
One equation, one guess. Hint: the □ symbol that packs wave propagation through spacetime into one neat object.
Commit to an answer in the comments, then swipe to see if you nailed it 👉

ANSWER: d'Alembert Operator

The wave operator ∂²/∂t² - c²∇² unifies spacetime in relativity! Also called the d'Alembertian, it describes how waves propagate through space and time with universal speed c.

daltons_law.name: Dalton's Law
daltons_law.caption:
Can you name this equation? Hint: add up each gas's own pressure to get the total — vital knowledge for scuba divers.
Guess in the comments before checking slide 2 — how many can you get? ✅

ANSWER: Dalton's Law

Total pressure = sum of partial pressures in gas mixtures! Dalton's law of partial pressures explains atmosphere composition and enables scuba diving calculations. Simple but profoundly useful.

darcy_law.name: Darcy's Law
darcy_law.caption:
Do you know this one? Hint: how groundwater seeps through soil and coffee drips through the grounds.
Say it out loud, drop it in the comments, then swipe to check 👉

ANSWER: Darcy's Law

Flow through porous media: Q = -KA(dh/dx) governs groundwater movement, soil drainage, and filtration. Darcy's law explains why some soils stay wet while others drain quickly.

darcy_weisbach_equation.name: Darcy-Weisbach Equation
darcy_weisbach_equation.caption:
Name that equation. Hint: how much pressure a pipeline loses to friction — engineers size pumps with it.
Comment your guess before you swipe — bonus points for the units 🤓

ANSWER: Darcy-Weisbach Equation

Friction loss in pipes: hf = f(L/D)(V²/2g) calculates pressure drop in fluid flow. Essential for pipeline design, engineers use this daily to size pumps and predict flow rates.

de_broglie_relation.name: de Broglie Relation
de_broglie_relation.caption:
What's this equation called? Hint: every moving particle has a wavelength — technically, even you do.
Drop your guess in the comments before you swipe — no peeking 👉

ANSWER: de Broglie Relation

λ = h/p: Matter has wavelength! de Broglie's insight revolutionized physics—electrons, atoms, and molecules exhibit wave behavior. This bridges the quantum and classical worlds fundamentally.

digamma_function.name: Digamma Function
digamma_function.caption:
Know this one? Hint: take the logarithm of the factorial's famous generalisation, then differentiate.
Comment your answer first, then swipe to slide 2 to check yourself 👉

ANSWER: Digamma Function

The logarithmic derivative of the gamma function, the digamma function appears in complex analysis, statistics, and quantum field theory. Pure mathematical elegance underlying physics.

distance_modulus.name: Distance Modulus
distance_modulus.caption:
Today's challenge: Hint: how astronomers turn 'how bright it looks' into 'how far away it is'.
Type your guess below before you swipe — no cheating, we'll know 👀

ANSWER: Distance Modulus

Astronomers measure cosmic distances using the distance modulus relating apparent and absolute magnitude! This equation connects observations of distant supernovae to universe expansion and dark energy.

doppler_effect.name: Doppler Effect
doppler_effect.caption:
Guess the equation. Hint: why a siren drops in pitch as it passes — and why distant galaxies look red.
Commit to an answer in the comments, then swipe to see if you nailed it 👉

ANSWER: Doppler Effect

Observer motion changes observed frequency—sirens pitch-shift as they pass, light from moving stars shifts red or blue. The Doppler effect reveals motion in the universe and powers velocity measurements.

drag_equation.name: Drag Equation
drag_equation.caption:
One equation, one guess. Hint: air resistance grows with speed squared — it's why skydivers stop accelerating.
Guess in the comments before checking slide 2 — how many can you get? ✅

ANSWER: Drag Equation

F_drag = 0.5ρv²ACD: Air resistance rises with the square of velocity! This equation explains why skydivers reach terminal velocity and why aerodynamic design matters for speed and efficiency.

drift_velocity.name: Drift Velocity
drift_velocity.caption:
Can you name this equation? Hint: electrons in your wires crawl at millimetres per second — this is their speed.
Say it out loud, drop it in the comments, then swipe to check 👉

ANSWER: Drift Velocity

Electrons in wires move surprisingly slowly—millimeters per second! Despite this, electrical signals propagate near light speed due to field effects. Drift velocity reconciles everyday electricity with physics.

einstein_field_equations_no_lambda_v1.name: Einstein Field Equations (No Lambda, Version 1)
einstein_field_equations_no_lambda_v1.caption:
Do you know this one? Hint: mass tells spacetime how to curve; spacetime tells mass how to move.
Comment your guess before you swipe — bonus points for the units 🤓

ANSWER: Einstein Field Equations (No Lambda)

Gμν = 8πGTμν: Einstein's masterpiece relates spacetime curvature to mass-energy distribution. Without dark energy, this equation describes gravity and the universe's large-scale structure.

einstein_field_equations_no_lambda_v2.name: Einstein Field Equations (No Lambda, Version 2)
einstein_field_equations_no_lambda_v2.caption:
Name that equation. Hint: the tensor equations that replaced Newton's gravity with curved spacetime.
Drop your guess in the comments before you swipe — no peeking 👉

ANSWER: Einstein Field Equations (No Lambda)

Different notation, same revolutionary insight: gravity emerges from curved spacetime! Einstein's equations are nature's blueprint, explaining black holes, gravitational waves, and the universe's fate.

einstein_field_equations_with_lambda_v1.name: Einstein Field Equations (With Lambda, Version 1)
einstein_field_equations_with_lambda_v1.caption:
What's this equation called? Hint: curved spacetime plus one extra constant — the term behind dark energy.
Comment your answer first, then swipe to slide 2 to check yourself 👉

ANSWER: Einstein Field Equations (With Lambda)

Add the cosmological constant Λ, and suddenly dark energy appears! Modern cosmology hinges on this term—explaining the accelerating expansion of our universe and its eventual heat death.

einstein_field_equations_with_lambda_v2.name: Einstein Field Equations (With Lambda, Version 2)
einstein_field_equations_with_lambda_v2.caption:
Know this one? Hint: its author called this constant his 'biggest blunder' — now it runs the cosmos.
Type your guess below before you swipe — no cheating, we'll know 👀

ANSWER: Einstein Field Equations (With Lambda)

The universe's ultimate fate rests on this one constant! The cosmological constant Λ drives acceleration, and understanding it might be the key to unifying quantum mechanics and gravity.

einstein_relation_diffusion.name: Einstein Relation (Diffusion)
einstein_relation_diffusion.caption:
Today's challenge: Hint: how pollen's random jiggling in water helped prove that atoms are real.
Commit to an answer in the comments, then swipe to see if you nailed it 👉

ANSWER: Einstein Relation (Diffusion)

D = kT/6πηr connects diffusion coefficient to molecular properties! Einstein showed random molecular motion leads to diffusion—a bridge between Brownian motion and thermodynamics.

electrical_power.name: Electrical Power
electrical_power.caption:
Guess the equation. Hint: the rate a circuit turns current into heat and light — your kettle obeys it.
Guess in the comments before checking slide 2 — how many can you get? ✅

ANSWER: Electrical Power

P = IV = I²R = V²/R: Multiple forms of one fundamental equation describing power dissipation in circuits. Understanding this equation is crucial for electronics, power systems, and energy efficiency.

energy_momentum_relation.name: Energy-Momentum Relation
energy_momentum_relation.caption:
One equation, one guess. Hint: special relativity's full upgrade of E = mc², with an extra term for motion.
Say it out loud, drop it in the comments, then swipe to check 👉

ANSWER: Energy-Momentum Relation

E² = (pc)² + (mc²)²: The relativistic relation between energy and momentum reveals rest mass energy mc²! Without this, there's no understanding of particle physics or nuclear power.

enthalpy.name: Enthalpy
enthalpy.caption:
Can you name this equation? Hint: the heat content chemists track in every reaction at constant pressure.
Comment your guess before you swipe — bonus points for the units 🤓

ANSWER: Enthalpy

H = U + PV: Enthalpy measures the total heat content of a system at constant pressure. Chemical reactions are described by enthalpy changes, making this crucial for chemistry and thermodynamics.

escape_velocity.name: Escape Velocity
escape_velocity.caption:
Do you know this one? Hint: hit 11.2 km/s straight up and Earth can never pull you back.
Drop your guess in the comments before you swipe — no peeking 👉

ANSWER: Escape Velocity

v = √(2GM/r): The minimum speed needed to escape a celestial body's gravity. This equation explains why Earth's escape velocity is 11 km/s while the Moon's is just 2.4 km/s.

eulers_formula.name: Euler's Formula
eulers_formula.caption:
Name that equation. Hint: five fundamental constants in one line — often voted the most beautiful equation.
Comment your answer first, then swipe to slide 2 to check yourself 👉

ANSWER: Euler's Formula

e^(iπ) + 1 = 0: Perhaps mathematics' most beautiful equation! This connects five fundamental constants in one elegant relation, appearing throughout physics, engineering, and pure mathematics.

eulers_identity.name: Euler's Identity
eulers_identity.caption:
What's this equation called? Hint: how e^(iθ) secretly contains circles, sines and cosines.
Type your guess below before you swipe — no cheating, we'll know 👀

ANSWER: Euler's Identity

e^(iθ) = cos(θ) + i·sin(θ): This identity reveals the deep connection between exponentials and trigonometric functions—foundational for complex analysis, Fourier transforms, and quantum mechanics.

eyring_equation.name: Eyring Equation
eyring_equation.caption:
Know this one? Hint: reaction rates from transition-state theory — the quantum upgrade to Arrhenius.
Commit to an answer in the comments, then swipe to see if you nailed it 👉

ANSWER: Eyring Equation

Eyring's absolute reaction rate theory provides an alternative to Arrhenius for chemical kinetics. It incorporates quantum mechanics and transition state theory—more sophisticated and physically revealing.

faradays_law_v1.name: Faraday's Law (Version 1)
faradays_law_v1.caption:
Today's challenge: Hint: wiggle a magnet near a coil and electricity appears — every generator's secret.
Guess in the comments before checking slide 2 — how many can you get? ✅

ANSWER: Faraday's Law

Changing magnetic flux induces electric fields! Faraday's law powers electric generators, transformers, and induction cooktops. It's one of Maxwell's four pillars of electromagnetism.

faradays_law_v2.name: Faraday's Law (Version 2)
faradays_law_v2.caption:
Guess the equation. Hint: the loop-and-flux form behind transformers, MRI machines and wireless charging.
Say it out loud, drop it in the comments, then swipe to check 👉

ANSWER: Faraday's Law

The integral form reveals magnetic flux through loops induces circulating electric fields. This understanding enables wireless power, MRI machines, and transformers worldwide.

fick_first_law.name: Fick's First Law
fick_first_law.caption:
One equation, one guess. Hint: why perfume crosses a room: stuff flows from crowded regions to empty ones.
Comment your guess before you swipe — bonus points for the units 🤓

ANSWER: Fick's First Law

Diffusion flux is proportional to concentration gradient: J = -D∇C. Fick's elegant law describes spreading of perfume, heat conduction analog, and molecular motion fundamentals.

fick_second_law.name: Fick's Second Law
fick_second_law.caption:
Can you name this equation? Hint: how an ink drop's concentration profile spreads and smooths itself over time.
Drop your guess in the comments before you swipe — no peeking 👉

ANSWER: Fick's Second Law

∂C/∂t = D∇²C describes how concentration evolves—the diffusion equation! Used everywhere from semiconductor processing to pollutant dispersion, it's fundamental to transport phenomena.

first_law_thermodynamics_v1.name: First Law of Thermodynamics (Version 1)
first_law_thermodynamics_v1.caption:
Do you know this one? Hint: heat in minus work out — the reason perpetual motion machines always fail.
Type your guess below before you swipe — no cheating, we'll know 👀

ANSWER: First Law of Thermodynamics

ΔU = Q - W: Energy conservation in thermodynamic form! Internal energy change equals heat added minus work done. This law explains why perpetual motion machines are impossible.

first_law_thermodynamics_v2.name: First Law of Thermodynamics (Version 2)
first_law_thermodynamics_v2.caption:
Name that equation. Hint: the infinitesimal bookkeeping of heat and work — conservation, one dU at a time.
Type your guess below before you swipe — no cheating, we'll know 👀

ANSWER: First Law of Thermodynamics

dU = δQ - δW: The differential form emphasizes infinitesimal energy changes. This microscopic perspective connects thermodynamics to statistical mechanics and molecular motion.

fouriers_law.name: Fourier's Law
fouriers_law.caption:
What's this equation called? Hint: why a metal spoon in hot soup burns your fingers — heat runs down the gradient.
Commit to an answer in the comments, then swipe to see if you nailed it 👉

ANSWER: Fourier's Law

Heat flow is proportional to temperature gradient: q = -k∇T. Fourier's law governs heat conduction in buildings, electronics cooling, and explains why metal spoons burn your hand.

free_fall_equation.name: Free Fall Equation
free_fall_equation.caption:
Know this one? Hint: drop a hammer and a feather on the Moon and they land together — this says why.
Guess in the comments before checking slide 2 — how many can you get? ✅

ANSWER: Free Fall Equation

h = 0.5gt²: Objects accelerate downward at g regardless of mass! Galileo's insight that all objects fall equally revolutionized physics and led to the principle of equivalence.

gamma_function.name: Gamma Function
gamma_function.caption:
Today's challenge: Hint: the factorial, extended to fractions and complex numbers.
Say it out loud, drop it in the comments, then swipe to check 👉

ANSWER: Gamma Function

Γ(n) = (n-1)! for integers, extended to all complex numbers: the gamma function generalizes factorials! It appears in probability distributions, quantum field theory, and special functions.

gauss_law_electricity_integral.name: Gauss' Law - Electricity (Integral Form)
gauss_law_electricity_integral.caption:
Guess the equation. Hint: count the flux leaving any closed surface and you've counted the charge inside.
Comment your guess before you swipe — bonus points for the units 🤓

ANSWER: Gauss' Law - Electricity (Integral Form)

Electrical flux through a closed surface equals enclosed charge divided by ε₀. Gauss' law simplifies calculations of electric fields—one of Maxwell's four fundamental equations.

gauss_law_electricity_v1.name: Gauss' Law - Electricity (Version 1)
gauss_law_electricity_v1.caption:
One equation, one guess. Hint: the local rule that says electric field lines are born on charges.
Drop your guess in the comments before you swipe — no peeking 👉

ANSWER: Gauss' Law - Electricity

∇·E = ρ/ε₀: The divergence of electric field is proportional to charge density. This differential form reveals how charges create electric fields locally, point by point.

gauss_law_electricity_v2.name: Gauss' Law - Electricity (Version 2)
gauss_law_electricity_v2.caption:
Can you name this equation? Hint: field lines start on positive charge and end on negative — stated point by point.
Comment your answer first, then swipe to slide 2 to check yourself 👉

ANSWER: Gauss' Law - Electricity

Different notation, same profound insight: electric field source is electric charge. This equation underlies everything from atomic structure to capacitors to atmospheric electricity.

gauss_law_electricity_v3.name: Gauss' Law - Electricity (Version 3)
gauss_law_electricity_v3.caption:
Do you know this one? Hint: the potential-flavoured way of saying charges shape the field around them.
Type your guess below before you swipe — no cheating, we'll know 👀

ANSWER: Gauss' Law - Electricity

Yet another form of Gauss' law showing charge distribution creates electric potential. No matter which form, the underlying physics remains: charges are sources of electric fields.

gauss_law_gravity_v1.name: Gauss' Law - Gravity (Version 1)
gauss_law_gravity_v1.caption:
Name that equation. Hint: electrostatics' famous flux law, rewritten with mass as the source.
Commit to an answer in the comments, then swipe to see if you nailed it 👉

ANSWER: Gauss' Law - Gravity

Gravitational analog to electric Gauss' law: gravitational flux equals enclosed mass. This elegant formulation shows gravity and electromagnetism share deep mathematical structure.

gauss_law_gravity_v2.name: Gauss' Law - Gravity (Version 2)
gauss_law_gravity_v2.caption:
What's this equation called? Hint: the divergence form: mass density tells you how strongly field lines converge.
Guess in the comments before checking slide 2 — how many can you get? ✅

ANSWER: Gauss' Law - Gravity

∇·g = 4πGρ: The divergence of gravitational field is proportional to mass density. This differential form enables calculating gravitational fields from any mass distribution.

gauss_law_magnetism_differential_v1.name: Gauss' Law - Magnetism (Differential, Version 1)
gauss_law_magnetism_differential_v1.caption:
Know this one? Hint: the four-symbol statement that magnetic monopoles don't exist.
Say it out loud, drop it in the comments, then swipe to check 👉

ANSWER: Gauss' Law - Magnetism (Differential)

∇·B = 0: Magnetic field divergence is zero everywhere—no magnetic monopoles exist in nature! This asymmetry between electricity and magnetism hints at deeper physics.

gauss_law_magnetism_differential_v2.name: Gauss' Law - Magnetism (Differential, Version 2)
gauss_law_magnetism_differential_v2.caption:
Today's challenge: Hint: why every magnetic field line loops back on itself — no starts, no ends.
Comment your guess before you swipe — bonus points for the units 🤓

ANSWER: Gauss' Law - Magnetism (Differential)

No magnetic charges anywhere: magnetic field lines form closed loops with no beginning or end. This conservation of magnetic flux underpins electromagnetic phenomena throughout nature.

gauss_law_magnetism_integral.name: Gauss' Law - Magnetism (Integral Form)
gauss_law_magnetism_integral.caption:
Guess the equation. Hint: total magnetic flux out of ANY closed surface: always exactly zero.
Drop your guess in the comments before you swipe — no peeking 👉

ANSWER: Gauss' Law - Magnetism (Integral Form)

Magnetic flux through any closed surface equals zero—direct consequence of no monopoles. This constraint shapes magnetic field topologies and enables magnetic confinement in fusion reactors.

gravitational_force_v1.name: Gravitational Force (Version 1)
gravitational_force_v1.caption:
One equation, one guess. Hint: the inverse-square attraction that keeps the Moon on its leash.
Comment your answer first, then swipe to slide 2 to check yourself 👉

ANSWER: Gravitational Force

F = GMm/r²: Newton's inverse-square law of gravity! Two masses attract proportionally to their product and inversely to distance squared. It explained planetary orbits and changed science forever.

gravitational_force_v2.name: Gravitational Force (Version 2)
gravitational_force_v2.caption:
Can you name this equation? Hint: the same famous attraction, recast as a field filling all of space.
Type your guess below before you swipe — no cheating, we'll know 👀

ANSWER: Gravitational Force

From Newton's law comes the gravitational field and potential energy. This formulation connects forces to fields, laying groundwork for Einstein's geometric interpretation of gravity.

gyroscopic_precession.name: Gyroscopic Precession
gyroscopic_precession.caption:
Do you know this one? Hint: push a spinning wheel and it tilts sideways instead — bicycles exploit it.
Commit to an answer in the comments, then swipe to see if you nailed it 👉

ANSWER: Gyroscopic Precession

Spinning objects precess when a torque is applied perpendicular to their spin axis! Gyroscopes stabilize bicycles and spacecraft—angular momentum's resistance to change in action.

hagen_poiseuille_equation.name: Hagen-Poiseuille Equation
hagen_poiseuille_equation.caption:
Name that equation. Hint: halve a pipe's radius and the flow drops 16-fold — arteries live by this.
Guess in the comments before checking slide 2 — how many can you get? ✅

ANSWER: Hagen-Poiseuille Equation

Flow rate through a pipe depends on the fourth power of radius! Q = πr⁴ΔP/(8ηL) explains why arteries need larger diameters for adequate blood flow and why pipelines require specific dimensions.

half_life.name: Half-Life
half_life.caption:
What's this equation called? Hint: the fixed tick of radioactive decay that lets us date Egyptian mummies.
Say it out loud, drop it in the comments, then swipe to check 👉

ANSWER: Half-Life

Radioactive nuclei decay exponentially with characteristic half-life—time for half to decay. Half-life enables radiometric dating of ancient samples and medical applications of isotopes.

half_life_copy.name: Half-Life (Second Form)
half_life_copy.caption:
Know this one? Hint: the exponential-decay clock ticking inside every unstable nucleus.
Comment your guess before you swipe — bonus points for the units 🤓

ANSWER: Half-Life (Second Form)

The exponential decay form reveals how quickly radioactive material reduces to half its initial amount. Applications range from carbon dating archaeology to medical imaging.

harish_chandra_formula.name: Harish-Chandra Formula
harish_chandra_formula.caption:
Today's challenge: Hint: deep representation theory that sneaks into particle-physics calculations.
Drop your guess in the comments before you swipe — no peeking 👉

ANSWER: Harish-Chandra Formula

Advanced representation theory formula appearing in quantum field theory and high-energy physics. This formula connects group theory to particle physics calculations—abstract mathematics meets experiment.

hawking_temperature.name: Hawking Temperature
hawking_temperature.caption:
Guess the equation. Hint: black holes glow — and the smaller they are, the hotter they burn.
Comment your answer first, then swipe to slide 2 to check yourself 👉

ANSWER: Hawking Temperature

Black holes aren't black—they emit radiation with temperature inversely proportional to mass! Hawking's discovery fundamentally changed black hole physics and united gravity with quantum mechanics.

heisenberg_uncertainty_energy_time.name: Heisenberg Uncertainty - Energy/Time
heisenberg_uncertainty_energy_time.caption:
One equation, one guess. Hint: the quantum trade-off that lets 'virtual' particles briefly borrow from the vacuum.
Type your guess below before you swipe — no cheating, we'll know 👀

ANSWER: Heisenberg Uncertainty - Energy/Time

ΔE·Δt ≥ ℏ/2: You cannot simultaneously know exact energy and exact time of measurement! This uncertainty principle underlies quantum mechanics—nature's fundamental limit on precision.

heisenberg_uncertainty_position_momentum.name: Heisenberg Uncertainty - Position/Momentum
heisenberg_uncertainty_position_momentum.caption:
Can you name this equation? Hint: pin down where a particle is, and you lose track of where it's going.
Commit to an answer in the comments, then swipe to see if you nailed it 👉

ANSWER: Heisenberg Uncertainty - Position/Momentum

Δx·Δp ≥ ℏ/2: More precisely you know position, less you know momentum and vice versa. This trade-off is quantum mechanics' most famous uncertainty—it's not measurement error but nature itself!

helmholtz_free_energy_v1.name: Helmholtz Free Energy (Version 1)
helmholtz_free_energy_v1.caption:
Do you know this one? Hint: the useful work you can extract at constant temperature and volume.
Guess in the comments before checking slide 2 — how many can you get? ✅

ANSWER: Helmholtz Free Energy

F = U - TS: At constant temperature and volume, free energy decreases as reactions proceed. Helmholtz free energy is crucial for chemical equilibrium and thermodynamic predictions.

helmholtz_free_energy_v2.name: Helmholtz Free Energy (Version 2)
helmholtz_free_energy_v2.caption:
Name that equation. Hint: its differential form ties S, T, P and V into one tidy statement.
Say it out loud, drop it in the comments, then swipe to check 👉

ANSWER: Helmholtz Free Energy

The differential form dF = -SdT - PdV connects entropy, temperature, pressure, and volume. This reveals thermodynamic relationships for systems at constant temperature.

hookes_law.name: Hooke's Law
hookes_law.caption:
What's this equation called? Hint: stretch a spring twice as far, feel exactly twice the pull back.
Comment your guess before you swipe — bonus points for the units 🤓

ANSWER: Hooke's Law

F = -kx: Force on a spring is proportional to displacement! Hooke's law explains elastic behavior of springs, rubber bands, and materials—fundamental to mechanical engineering and physics.

ideal_gas_law.name: Ideal Gas Law
ideal_gas_law.caption:
Know this one? Hint: P, V, T and n in one line — chemistry's single most-used equation.
Drop your guess in the comments before you swipe — no peeking 👉

ANSWER: Ideal Gas Law

PV = nRT: The most important equation in chemistry and thermodynamics! This relates pressure, volume, temperature, and amount of gas—explaining behavior of air, ideal gases, and more.

impulse_v1.name: Impulse (Version 1)
impulse_v1.caption:
Today's challenge: Hint: force × time — why airbags save lives by stretching out the crash.
Comment your answer first, then swipe to slide 2 to check yourself 👉

ANSWER: Impulse

Impulse J = F·Δt equals change in momentum. A force applied for longer creates more impulse—explaining why airbags save lives by extending collision time.

impulse_v2.name: Impulse (Version 2)
impulse_v2.caption:
Guess the equation. Hint: the integral of force over time — how rockets bank up their momentum change.
Type your guess below before you swipe — no cheating, we'll know 👀

ANSWER: Impulse

The integral form J = ∫F dt reveals impulse as accumulated force over time. Rockets, explosions, and sports involve impulse—understanding it is key to controlling motion.

jurin_law_capillary_rise.name: Jurin's Law - Capillary Rise
jurin_law_capillary_rise.caption:
One equation, one guess. Hint: why water climbs a narrow glass tube — and how trees drink.
Commit to an answer in the comments, then swipe to see if you nailed it 👉

ANSWER: Jurin's Law - Capillary Rise

h = 2σcosθ/(ρgr): Capillary rise height depends on surface tension and tube radius! Jurin's law explains why water climbs narrow tubes and how plants transport water via capillary action.

kepler_third_law.name: Kepler's Third Law
kepler_third_law.caption:
Can you name this equation? Hint: square a planet's year, cube its distance — the ratio never changes.
Guess in the comments before checking slide 2 — how many can you get? ✅

ANSWER: Kepler's Third Law

T² ∝ a³: Orbital period squared is proportional to semi-major axis cubed! Kepler's elegant law describes planetary orbits and enabled Newton to derive his gravity law.

kinetic_energy.name: Kinetic Energy
kinetic_energy.caption:
Do you know this one? Hint: double a car's speed and its crash punch quadruples.
Say it out loud, drop it in the comments, then swipe to check 👉

ANSWER: Kinetic Energy

KE = 0.5mv²: Moving objects possess kinetic energy proportional to mass and velocity squared. This fundamental form of energy powers everything from molecules to galaxies.

kronecker_delta.name: Kronecker Delta
kronecker_delta.caption:
Name that equation. Hint: the tiny two-index switch: 1 when they match, 0 when they don't.
Comment your guess before you swipe — bonus points for the units 🤓

ANSWER: Kronecker Delta

δij = 1 if i=j, else 0: This simple but powerful function appears throughout mathematics, physics, and engineering for index calculations and orthogonality conditions.

lane_emden_equation.name: Lane-Emden Equation
lane_emden_equation.caption:
What's this equation called? Hint: the ODE that sculpts the insides of stars held up against their own gravity.
Drop your guess in the comments before you swipe — no peeking 👉

ANSWER: Lane-Emden Equation

This differential equation describes the structure of self-gravitating, polytropic spheres like stars! The Lane-Emden equation is fundamental to stellar structure theory and astrophysics.

langevin_equation.name: Langevin Equation
langevin_equation.caption:
Know this one? Hint: Newton's second law with a random kick — pollen grains dancing in water.
Comment your answer first, then swipe to slide 2 to check yourself 👉

ANSWER: Langevin Equation

m(dv/dt) = F - γv + f(t): This stochastic differential equation describes Brownian motion, friction, and random forces! Essential for understanding molecular dynamics and statistical mechanics.

laplace_equation_v1.name: Laplace's Equation (Version 1)
laplace_equation_v1.caption:
Today's challenge: Hint: the source-free equation every steady temperature map and potential obeys.
Type your guess below before you swipe — no cheating, we'll know 👀

ANSWER: Laplace's Equation

∇²φ = 0: Laplace's equation describes steady-state potential fields with no sources. It appears in electrostatics, gravity, and heat flow—one of physics' most important PDEs.

laplace_equation_v2.name: Laplace's Equation (Version 2)
laplace_equation_v2.caption:
Guess the equation. Hint: smooth, source-free and everywhere in physics — the harmonic condition.
Commit to an answer in the comments, then swipe to see if you nailed it 👉

ANSWER: Laplace's Equation

Different notation, same powerful equation: Laplacian of potential vanishes everywhere. Solving Laplace's equation with boundary conditions unlocks countless physics problems.

laplace_transform.name: Laplace Transform
laplace_transform.caption:
One equation, one guess. Hint: it turns nasty differential equations into plain algebra — engineers swear by it.
Guess in the comments before checking slide 2 — how many can you get? ✅

ANSWER: Laplace Transform

F(s) = ∫₀^∞ e^(-st) f(t) dt: The Laplace transform converts differential equations to algebraic form! Indispensable for control theory, electrical engineering, and solving ODEs.

lorentz_force_v1.name: Lorentz Force (Version 1)
lorentz_force_v1.caption:
Can you name this equation? Hint: the sideways shove a magnetic field gives a moving charge.
Say it out loud, drop it in the comments, then swipe to check 👉

ANSWER: Lorentz Force

F = q(E + v × B): Charged particles experience forces from electric and magnetic fields! The Lorentz force governs particle motion in plasma, accelerators, and magnetospheres.

lorentz_force_v2.name: Lorentz Force (Version 2)
lorentz_force_v2.caption:
Do you know this one? Hint: how electric and magnetic fields team up to steer beams of charged particles.
Comment your guess before you swipe — bonus points for the units 🤓

ANSWER: Lorentz Force

The field form reveals how electromagnetic fields create forces on charged particles. Lorentz force is the bridge connecting field theory to particle dynamics.

luminosity_v1.name: Luminosity (Version 1)
luminosity_v1.caption:
Name that equation. Hint: a star's total power output, worked out from its brightness and distance.
Drop your guess in the comments before you swipe — no peeking 👉

ANSWER: Luminosity

L = 4πr²F: Luminosity is the total power radiated by a star, calculated from flux and distance! Understanding stellar luminosity unlocks the HR diagram and stellar evolution.

luminosity_v2.name: Luminosity (Version 2)
luminosity_v2.caption:
What's this equation called? Hint: the 'standard candle' quantity that exposed the universe's accelerating expansion.
Comment your answer first, then swipe to slide 2 to check yourself 👉

ANSWER: Luminosity

Different forms relate luminosity to intrinsic star properties and observed brightness. Measuring luminosity distances to supernovae revealed the universe's accelerating expansion!

majorana_equation.name: Majorana Equation
majorana_equation.caption:
Know this one? Hint: it predicts particles that are their own antiparticles.
Type your guess below before you swipe — no cheating, we'll know 👀

ANSWER: Majorana Equation

Describing massive neutrinos and exotic particles, the Majorana equation is a fundamental equation in quantum field theory. It predicts particles that are their own antiparticles!

malus_law.name: Malus' Law
malus_law.caption:
Today's challenge: Hint: why two polarising filters at 90° block light completely.
Commit to an answer in the comments, then swipe to see if you nailed it 👉

ANSWER: Malus' Law

I = I₀cos²θ: Intensity of polarized light through a polarizer depends on angle squared! Malus' law explains sunglasses, LCD screens, and quantum measurement foundations.

mass_energy_equivalence.name: Mass-Energy Equivalence
mass_energy_equivalence.caption:
Guess the equation. Hint: the most famous equation on Earth — three symbols that started the atomic age.
Guess in the comments before checking slide 2 — how many can you get? ✅

ANSWER: Mass-Energy Equivalence

E = mc²: Energy and mass are interchangeable! Einstein's most famous equation unified matter and energy, explaining nuclear power, particle creation, and the universe's evolution.

moment_of_inertia.name: Moment of Inertia
moment_of_inertia.caption:
One equation, one guess. Hint: rotation's stand-in for mass — why a flywheel resists being spun up.
Say it out loud, drop it in the comments, then swipe to check 👉

ANSWER: Moment of Inertia

I = ∫r²dm: Moment of inertia measures how mass is distributed relative to a rotation axis. It's the rotational analog of mass in Newton's second law for rotations.

newton_law_viscosity.name: Newton's Law of Viscosity
newton_law_viscosity.caption:
Can you name this equation? Hint: why honey pours slowly: shear stress tracks how fast the layers slide.
Comment your guess before you swipe — bonus points for the units 🤓

ANSWER: Newton's Law of Viscosity

Shear stress is proportional to strain rate: τ = μ(du/dy). This law defines Newtonian fluids and enables calculating viscous forces in pipe flow, lubrication, and blood flow.

partition_function.name: Partition Function
partition_function.caption:
Do you know this one? Hint: the single sum Z from which ALL of thermodynamics can be derived.
Drop your guess in the comments before you swipe — no peeking 👉

ANSWER: Partition Function

Z = Σe^(-βEᵢ): The partition function encodes all statistical information about a system! From it, you can derive energy, entropy, and all thermodynamic properties.

photoelectric_effect.name: Photoelectric Effect
photoelectric_effect.caption:
Name that equation. Hint: light knocks electrons off metal — the discovery that won Einstein his Nobel.
Comment your answer first, then swipe to slide 2 to check yourself 👉

ANSWER: Photoelectric Effect

E = hf: Light hits metal and ejects electrons—energy comes in photons! Einstein's explanation of the photoelectric effect won him the Nobel Prize and launched quantum mechanics.

photon_energy.name: Photon Energy
photon_energy.caption:
What's this equation called? Hint: why blue light packs more punch per particle than red.
Type your guess below before you swipe — no cheating, we'll know 👀

ANSWER: Photon Energy

E = hf = hc/λ: Photon energy depends on frequency or inversely on wavelength! This equation connects particle properties (energy) to wave properties (frequency).

poisson_equation_v1.name: Poisson's Equation (Version 1)
poisson_equation_v1.caption:
Know this one? Hint: Laplace's equation with a source term switched on.
Commit to an answer in the comments, then swipe to see if you nailed it 👉

ANSWER: Poisson's Equation

∇²φ = ρ: Poisson's equation extends Laplace's with source terms! It governs electrostatic potential from charge distribution and gravitational potential from mass.

poisson_equation_v2.name: Poisson's Equation (Version 2)
poisson_equation_v2.caption:
Today's challenge: Hint: how a charge or mass distribution sculpts the potential around it.
Guess in the comments before checking slide 2 — how many can you get? ✅

ANSWER: Poisson's Equation

Different form, same fundamental equation linking Laplacian to source: Poisson's equation is essential for electrostatics and gravity calculations with localized sources.

pop_sixth_derivative.name: Pop (Sixth Derivative)
pop_sixth_derivative.caption:
Guess the equation. Hint: position differentiated six times — snap and crackle's little sibling.
Say it out loud, drop it in the comments, then swipe to check 👉

ANSWER: Pop (Sixth Derivative)

Pop is the sixth time derivative of position—beyond crackle! While rarely used in physics, higher derivatives showcase calculus' power and appear in robotics and advanced control systems.

potential_flow.name: Potential Flow
potential_flow.caption:
One equation, one guess. Hint: when a fluid's entire motion is just the gradient of one scalar field.
Comment your guess before you swipe — bonus points for the units 🤓

ANSWER: Potential Flow

Flow where velocity is the gradient of a potential: v = ∇φ. Potential flow simplifies fluid dynamics calculations and works well for inviscid, irrotational flows.

poynting_vector.name: Poynting Vector
poynting_vector.caption:
Can you name this equation? Hint: E × B — it literally points where electromagnetic energy is going.
Drop your guess in the comments before you swipe — no peeking 👉

ANSWER: Poynting Vector

S = E × B: The Poynting vector describes electromagnetic energy flux and direction! It shows how energy flows with electromagnetic waves—crucial for understanding light and radio.

pressure_head.name: Pressure Head
pressure_head.caption:
Do you know this one? Hint: how engineers state a pipe's push as an equivalent column of water.
Comment your answer first, then swipe to slide 2 to check yourself 👉

ANSWER: Pressure Head

h = P/(ρg): Pressure converted to equivalent height of fluid column! Pressure head is useful for visualizing pressure in pipes and understanding hydraulic systems.

prony_equation.name: Prony Equation
prony_equation.caption:
Name that equation. Hint: the 1800s pipe-friction formula that Darcy-Weisbach eventually retired.
Type your guess below before you swipe — no cheating, we'll know 👀

ANSWER: Prony Equation

Ancient formula for estimating pipe friction: Prony's equation predates Darcy-Weisbach! Though largely superseded, it represents early engineering science connecting experiments to equations.

pythagorean_theorem.name: Pythagorean Theorem
pythagorean_theorem.caption:
What's this equation called? Hint: the right-triangle rule your GPS is quietly using right now.
Commit to an answer in the comments, then swipe to see if you nailed it 👉

ANSWER: Pythagorean Theorem

a² + b² = c²: The foundation of geometry for 2500 years! This theorem enables calculating distances, designing buildings, and is essential for physics from vectors to relativity.

quadratic_formula_v1.name: Quadratic Formula (Version 1)
quadratic_formula_v1.caption:
Know this one? Hint: the −b ± √... incantation every algebra student learns to chant.
Guess in the comments before checking slide 2 — how many can you get? ✅

ANSWER: Quadratic Formula

x = (-b ± √(b²-4ac)) / 2a: The most famous formula for solving quadratics! Essential tool in algebra, physics, and engineering for decades of students worldwide.

quadratic_formula_v2.name: Quadratic Formula (Version 2)
quadratic_formula_v2.caption:
Today's challenge: Hint: the discriminant-flavoured route to any parabola's roots.
Say it out loud, drop it in the comments, then swipe to check 👉

ANSWER: Quadratic Formula

Different forms of the same formula emphasize different mathematical aspects. The quadratic formula's elegance inspired generations of mathematicians and physicists.

quantum_momentum_operator.name: Quantum Momentum Operator
quantum_momentum_operator.caption:
Guess the equation. Hint: −iℏ∇ — apply it to a wavefunction and out comes p.
Comment your guess before you swipe — bonus points for the units 🤓

ANSWER: Quantum Momentum Operator

p̂ = -iℏ∇: The momentum operator in quantum mechanics lets you find particle momentum from the wavefunction! It's the foundation of quantum dynamics and the Schrödinger equation.

rayleigh_criterion.name: Rayleigh Criterion
rayleigh_criterion.caption:
One equation, one guess. Hint: the diffraction limit on how close two stars can sit before a telescope blurs them.
Drop your guess in the comments before you swipe — no peeking 👉

ANSWER: Rayleigh Criterion

θ = 1.22λ/D: Minimum angular separation to resolve two point sources depends on wavelength and aperture! The Rayleigh criterion limits telescope resolution and optical imaging.

rayleigh_jeans_law.name: Rayleigh-Jeans Law
rayleigh_jeans_law.caption:
Can you name this equation? Hint: the classical glow prediction that exploded into the ultraviolet catastrophe.
Comment your answer first, then swipe to slide 2 to check yourself 👉

ANSWER: Rayleigh-Jeans Law

Classical prediction for blackbody radiation—it fails catastrophically at high frequencies! The ultraviolet catastrophe drove Planck to quantum mechanics and changed physics forever.

redshift_equation.name: Redshift Equation
redshift_equation.caption:
Do you know this one? Hint: how far a galaxy's light has been stretched by the expanding universe.
Type your guess below before you swipe — no cheating, we'll know 👀

ANSWER: Redshift Equation

z = Δλ/λ: Cosmological redshift measures how much wavelengths stretched from expanding universe! Hubble's observations of redshift revealed the universe expands and led to the Big Bang.

riemann_silberstein_vector.name: Riemann-Silberstein Vector
riemann_silberstein_vector.caption:
Name that equation. Hint: E and B fused into one complex object, beloved of quantum optics.
Commit to an answer in the comments, then swipe to see if you nailed it 👉

ANSWER: Riemann-Silberstein Vector

Advanced electromagnetic theory construct combining electric and magnetic fields into a complex vector! Used in modern quantum optics and exotic light-matter interactions.

riemann_zeta_function_v1.name: Riemann Zeta Function (Version 1)
riemann_zeta_function_v1.caption:
What's this equation called? Hint: sum 1/nˢ forever — its zeros guard a $1,000,000 prize.
Guess in the comments before checking slide 2 — how many can you get? ✅

ANSWER: Riemann Zeta Function

ζ(s) = Σ(1/n^s) from n=1 to ∞: This function connects prime numbers to physics! The Riemann Hypothesis about its zeros might be the deepest unsolved problem in mathematics.

riemann_zeta_function_v2.name: Riemann Zeta Function (Version 2)
riemann_zeta_function_v2.caption:
Know this one? Hint: the critical-line mystery: mathematics' most wanted unsolved hypothesis.
Say it out loud, drop it in the comments, then swipe to check 👉

ANSWER: Riemann Zeta Function

The analytic continuation of the zeta function reveals zeros on the critical line—if the Riemann Hypothesis is true! These zeros might encode information about quantum chaos.

rogers_ramanujan_continued_fraction.name: Rogers-Ramanujan Continued Fraction
rogers_ramanujan_continued_fraction.caption:
Today's challenge: Hint: a nested tower of ratios — co-signed by India's self-taught mathematical genius.
Comment your guess before you swipe — bonus points for the units 🤓

ANSWER: Rogers-Ramanujan Continued Fraction

A beautiful continued fraction appearing in partition functions and modular forms! Named after Rogers and Ramanujan, it connects number theory to mathematical physics.

rotatum.name: Rotatum (Fourth Derivative)
rotatum.caption:
Guess the equation. Hint: how quickly a torque itself changes with time — rare, but real.
Drop your guess in the comments before you swipe — no peeking 👉

ANSWER: Rotatum (Fourth Derivative)

Rotatum is the fourth time derivative of position—also called snap! Though uncommon in physics, it appears in trajectory smoothing and high-precision spacecraft maneuvering.

rydberg_formula.name: Rydberg Formula
rydberg_formula.caption:
One equation, one guess. Hint: the recipe that predicts every line in hydrogen's spectrum.
Comment your answer first, then swipe to slide 2 to check yourself 👉

ANSWER: Rydberg Formula

1/λ = R(1/n₁² - 1/n₂²): This empirical formula predicts atomic spectral lines with stunning precision! It led to Bohr's model and demonstrated quantum mechanics' power.

scale_height.name: Scale Height
scale_height.caption:
Can you name this equation? Hint: the e-folding distance of an atmosphere — Everest is only about half of one.
Type your guess below before you swipe — no cheating, we'll know 👀

ANSWER: Scale Height

H = kT/(mg): Atmosphere scale height determines how fast pressure drops with altitude. Mount Everest is only ~0.5 scale heights high—dramatic effect on oxygen availability.

schrodinger_equation.name: Schrödinger Equation
schrodinger_equation.caption:
Do you know this one? Hint: the master equation for how a wavefunction evolves — cat included.
Commit to an answer in the comments, then swipe to see if you nailed it 👉

ANSWER: Schrödinger Equation

iℏ∂ψ/∂t = -ℏ²/2m ∇²ψ + Vψ: The cornerstone of quantum mechanics describes how wavefunctions evolve! Schrödinger's equation is as fundamental to quantum as Newton's is to classical mechanics.

schwarzschild_radius.name: Schwarzschild Radius
schwarzschild_radius.caption:
Name that equation. Hint: squeeze anything smaller than this and light itself can't get back out.
Guess in the comments before checking slide 2 — how many can you get? ✅

ANSWER: Schwarzschild Radius

rs = 2GM/c²: Collapse any mass into this radius and you create a black hole's event horizon! Schwarzschild radius reveals when gravity becomes so strong that not even light escapes.

shannon_entropy.name: Shannon Entropy
shannon_entropy.caption:
What's this equation called? Hint: the bit-counting measure of surprise that founded information theory.
Say it out loud, drop it in the comments, then swipe to check 👉

ANSWER: Shannon Entropy

H = -Σp(x)log₂p(x): Information theory's fundamental measure of uncertainty and data compression limits! Shannon entropy connects physics, mathematics, computer science, and biology.

simple_harmonic_motion.name: Simple Harmonic Motion
simple_harmonic_motion.caption:
Know this one? Hint: the −ω²x heartbeat of every pendulum, spring and vibrating atom.
Comment your guess before you swipe — bonus points for the units 🤓

ANSWER: Simple Harmonic Motion

a = -ω²x: Restoring force proportional to displacement creates oscillation! Simple harmonic motion appears everywhere—springs, pendulums, atoms—foundational to physics.

snap_fourth_derivative.name: Snap (Fourth Derivative)
snap_fourth_derivative.caption:
Today's challenge: Hint: position differentiated four times — first of a cereal-mascot trio.
Drop your guess in the comments before you swipe — no peeking 👉

ANSWER: Snap (Fourth Derivative)

Snap is the fourth time derivative of position! While snap is rarely used directly, it matters in comfort (avoiding jerky transitions) and robotics trajectory planning.

snells_law.name: Snell's Law
snells_law.caption:
Guess the equation. Hint: why a straw looks bent in a glass of water — light kinks between media.
Comment your answer first, then swipe to slide 2 to check yourself 👉

ANSWER: Snell's Law

n₁sinθ₁ = n₂sinθ₂: Light bends when entering different media! Snell's law explains rainbows, lenses, and fiber optic cables—governing light refraction universally.

standard_deviation.name: Standard Deviation
standard_deviation.caption:
One equation, one guess. Hint: the ± number that tells you how spread out your data really is.
Type your guess below before you swipe — no cheating, we'll know 👀

ANSWER: Standard Deviation

σ = √(Σ(x-μ)²/N): Measures spread of data around mean—how much variation exists! Standard deviation is crucial for statistics, error analysis, and understanding measurement uncertainty.

stefan_boltzmann_law.name: Stefan-Boltzmann Law
stefan_boltzmann_law.caption:
Can you name this equation? Hint: double a star's temperature and it radiates sixteen times the power.
Commit to an answer in the comments, then swipe to see if you nailed it 👉

ANSWER: Stefan-Boltzmann Law

P = σAT⁴: Total power radiated by object rises with absolute temperature to the fourth power! Stefan-Boltzmann law explains why hot objects glow, stars cool, and climate depends on temperature.

stokes_einstein_equation.name: Stokes-Einstein Equation
stokes_einstein_equation.caption:
Do you know this one? Hint: how a particle's size and the fluid's syrupiness set how fast it diffuses.
Guess in the comments before checking slide 2 — how many can you get? ✅

ANSWER: Stokes-Einstein Equation

D = kT/(6πηr): Connects diffusion coefficient to viscosity and particle size! This equation bridges molecular properties and macroscopic transport phenomena.

stokes_law_v1.name: Stokes' Law (Version 1)
stokes_law_v1.caption:
Name that equation. Hint: the viscous drag on a tiny sphere — why raindrops don't hit like bullets.
Say it out loud, drop it in the comments, then swipe to check 👉

ANSWER: Stokes' Law

F = 6πηrv: Drag force on a sphere in viscous fluid—Stokes' law explains raindrops' terminal velocity and particle settling! Used to measure viscosity and analyze colloidal systems.

stokes_law_v2.name: Stokes' Law (Version 2)
stokes_law_v2.caption:
What's this equation called? Hint: how fast a small sphere settles through a thick fluid.
Comment your guess before you swipe — bonus points for the units 🤓

ANSWER: Stokes' Law

Different form of Stokes' drag showing force dependence on radius and velocity. Essential for understanding particle motion in fluids and biological flows.

thermodynamic_entropy.name: Thermodynamic Entropy
thermodynamic_entropy.caption:
Know this one? Hint: δQ over T — the bookkeeping behind the arrow of time.
Drop your guess in the comments before you swipe — no peeking 👉

ANSWER: Thermodynamic Entropy

dS = δQ/T: Entropy change from heat transfer at temperature reveals spontaneity direction! Thermodynamic entropy predicts which processes occur naturally—the arrow of time.

toomre_stability_criterion.name: Toomre Stability Criterion
toomre_stability_criterion.caption:
Today's challenge: Hint: the Q that decides whether a galaxy's disc fragments into stars.
Comment your answer first, then swipe to slide 2 to check yourself 👉

ANSWER: Toomre Stability Criterion

Q = σ/(3.36Gμ): Measures disk stability against collapse—critical in galaxy and accretion disk physics! Toomre's criterion determines when flat structures fragment into stars and rings.

torque.name: Torque
torque.caption:
Guess the equation. Hint: why a longer wrench beats a stubborn bolt.
Type your guess below before you swipe — no cheating, we'll know 👀

ANSWER: Torque

τ = r × F: Torque measures rotational force—the rotational analog of force in linear motion! Torque governs everything from opening doors to planetary spin.

tsiolkovsky_rocket_equation.name: Tsiolkovsky Rocket Equation
tsiolkovsky_rocket_equation.caption:
One equation, one guess. Hint: the tyranny of the mass ratio — every space mission bows to it.
Commit to an answer in the comments, then swipe to see if you nailed it 👉

ANSWER: Tsiolkovsky Rocket Equation

Δv = vₑ ln(m₀/mf): Final velocity change depends on exhaust velocity and mass ratio! Tsiolkovsky's equation is the foundation of rocketry—every space mission follows it.

van_t_hoff_equation.name: van 't Hoff Equation
van_t_hoff_equation.caption:
Can you name this equation? Hint: how a reaction's equilibrium shifts when you turn up the heat.
Guess in the comments before checking slide 2 — how many can you get? ✅

ANSWER: van 't Hoff Equation

ln(K) = -ΔH/RT + ΔS/R: Relates equilibrium constant to temperature, enabling prediction of reaction equilibrium shifts! Essential for chemistry and biochemistry.

vis_viva_equation.name: Vis Viva Equation
vis_viva_equation.caption:
Do you know this one? Hint: orbital speed anywhere on an ellipse, from just r and a — the name is Latin.
Say it out loud, drop it in the comments, then swipe to check 👉

ANSWER: Vis Viva Equation

v² = GM(2/r - 1/a): Orbital velocity at any distance depends on semi-major axis! Vis viva (living force) equation simplifies orbital mechanics for any eccentricity.

vorticity_equation.name: Vorticity Equation
vorticity_equation.caption:
Name that equation. Hint: how swirls in a fluid stretch, tilt and spread — turbulence's bookkeeper.
Comment your guess before you swipe — bonus points for the units 🤓

ANSWER: Vorticity Equation

∂ω/∂t + (u·∇)ω = (ω·∇)u + ν∇²ω: Describes how fluid rotation evolves! Vorticity equation underlies turbulence modeling and complex fluid dynamics.

wave_equation_v1.name: Wave Equation (Version 1)
wave_equation_v1.caption:
What's this equation called? Hint: the one PDE that sound, light and water ripples all obey.
Drop your guess in the comments before you swipe — no peeking 👉

ANSWER: Wave Equation

∂²u/∂t² = c²∇²u: The wave equation describes propagation of waves through any medium! Sound, light, water—all obey this fundamental PDE governing wave phenomena.

wave_equation_v2.name: Wave Equation (Version 2)
wave_equation_v2.caption:
Know this one? Hint: the d'Alembertian form Einstein used for ripples in spacetime itself.
Comment your answer first, then swipe to slide 2 to check yourself 👉

ANSWER: Wave Equation

Different form emphasizing d'Alembertian operator: the wave equation unifies spacetime in relativity. Einstein used this equation to describe gravitational waves.

wien_displacement_law.name: Wien Displacement Law
wien_displacement_law.caption:
Today's challenge: Hint: why red-hot is cooler than white-hot — peak colour tracks temperature.
Type your guess below before you swipe — no cheating, we'll know 👀

ANSWER: Wien Displacement Law

λₘₐₓ = b/T: Peak wavelength of blackbody radiation shifts with temperature! Wien's law explains why hot objects emit visible light while cool ones emit infrared.

work_energy_theorem.name: Work-Energy Theorem
work_energy_theorem.caption:
Guess the equation. Hint: push something along a distance and its ½mv² changes by exactly that amount.
Commit to an answer in the comments, then swipe to see if you nailed it 👉

ANSWER: Work-Energy Theorem

W = ΔKE: Work done equals change in kinetic energy! This fundamental theorem connects forces and motion, simplifying mechanics problems throughout classical physics.

young_laplace_equation.name: Young-Laplace Equation
young_laplace_equation.caption:
One equation, one guess. Hint: why small soap bubbles have higher pressure inside than big ones.
Guess in the comments before checking slide 2 — how many can you get? ✅

ANSWER: Young-Laplace Equation

ΔP = 2σ/r: Pressure difference across curved interface depends on surface tension and radius! Young-Laplace explains why small bubbles burst first and governs capillary phenomena.
